1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	---
2	name: blueprint-reviewer
3	description: Whole-blueprint audit. Per-chapter checklist of completeness + correctness plus summaries of which parts are incomplete, which proofs lack detail, whether Lean targets are well-formulated, and whether multi-route strategies have coverage for every route. Also audits STRATEGY.md phases for blueprint coverage gaps and proposes concrete chapter outlines for phases not yet started, so the plan agent can immediately dispatch a blueprint-writer rather than defer.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan, dag]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch me BEFORE writing any prover objectives or touching Lean files.
11	    The plan agent's job is to make sure the blueprint is complete and
12	    detailed enough first; only then should provers be assigned. A weak
13	    blueprint produces low-quality prover work that the next iter then
14	    has to throw away.
15	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. The audit warning fires
16	    if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
17	
18	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
19	      - no chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/` was edited since my
20	        prior dispatch (check via `git diff --stat HEAD~N
21	        blueprint/src/chapters/` where N spans iters back to my last
22	        run);
23	      - my prior verdict cleared the HARD GATE for all chapters
24	        currently under active prover work;
25	      - no must-fix-this-iter finding from my prior dispatch remains
26	        live (every flagged chapter has either been writer-patched or
27	        dropped from objectives).
28	
29	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
30	    with the one-line rationale. Do NOT skip me when the prior verdict
31	    flagged any chapter `partial | false` and that chapter still feeds
32	    a live prover lane — the HARD GATE depends on a current audit.
33	  - I always read the WHOLE blueprint. Do not pass me a scope-limiting
34	    directive — even when the iteration's focus is narrow, the cross-
35	    chapter view is the point of running me.
36	  - Read my per-chapter checklist and use it to decide which chapters
37	    need a follow-up writer dispatch this iter (consult your catalog
38	    for the blueprint-writing subagent). You do not need to re-read
39	    the chapters yourself; the checklist is your view into them.
40	  - **Act on my unstarted-phase proposals.** My report's
41	    `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals` section names every
42	    strategy phase with no blueprint coverage and provides a concrete
43	    chapter outline for each. These are not informational — treat
44	    each proposal as a blueprint-writer directive seed. Dispatch a
45	    blueprint-writing subagent for each proposed chapter this iter
46	    (or record an explicit deferral rationale in the iter sidecar).
47	    Do NOT let unstarted phases accumulate across iters: a phase
48	    with no blueprint is a phase that cannot be parallelised, cannot
49	    be reviewed, and cannot be handed to a prover. Writing the
50	    blueprint early is the cheapest action in the loop.
51	
52	  ### HARD GATE — per-file prover dispatch
53	
54	  This is the rule that protects the project from low-quality prover work.
55	  Apply it verbatim, every iter, no exceptions:
56	
57	  - For each `.lean` file F you are considering adding to
58	    `## Current Objectives` (i.e. about to send a prover to), identify
59	    the corresponding blueprint chapter C. The mapping is: if some
60	    chapter declares `% archon:covers ... F ...` near its top, THAT
61	    chapter is C (a consolidated chapter blueprints several files while
62	    its siblings are thin pointers); otherwise fall back to the 1:1
63	    `Foo/Bar.lean → Foo_Bar.tex` slug. Look up C in my per-chapter
64	    checklist. When a chapter declares `covers:`, its single verdict
65	    gates EVERY file it lists — so a `correct: false` on the
66	    consolidated chapter defers all of them, and one writer directive
67	    against that chapter fixes the content behind all of them.
68	  - If C has `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND no must-fix-this-iter
69	    finding touches it, F may go into the objectives.
70	  - Otherwise (C is `partial | false` on either axis, OR a must-fix
71	    finding names C, OR a broken `\uses{}` in C points at a label F's
72	    blueprint depends on):
73	    1. DROP F from this iter's objectives (by default — but see the
74	       same-iter fast path below).
75	    2. Dispatch the catalog's blueprint-writing subagent for C THIS
76	       iter with a directive targeting the specific must-fix items I
77	       flagged.
78	    3. Record in iter/iter-NNN/plan.md why F was at risk and what the
79	       writer was asked to fix.
80	  - **Same-iter fast path (sanctioned — removes a wasted iter).** After
81	    the writer returns AND `lake build` is green, you MAY re-dispatch me
82	    scoped to C alone — a fresh slug, a directive naming only C and the
83	    must-fix items I flagged. If that scoped re-review returns C as
84	    `complete: true` AND `correct: true` with no must-fix finding, the
85	    gate is satisfied for C: add F to THIS iter's objectives and send a
86	    prover. No need to burn an iter waiting. If the scoped re-review
87	    still fails, F stays deferred.
88	  - If you do NOT take the fast path, F simply waits: the next iter's
89	    mandatory dispatch of me re-confirms C before any prover runs on F.
90	    The fast path is purely a latency optimization — it never weakens
91	    the gate. F enters objectives ONLY on a fresh `complete + correct`
92	    verdict with no must-fix, whether that verdict arrives this iter (via
93	    the fast path) or next (via the mandatory dispatch). A green build
94	    alone is NOT sufficient; the re-review is what clears the gate.
95	
96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.

116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Dependency-graph integrity (use `leandag`)** — the `\uses{}` edges must reflect the real mathematical dependencies. `leandag` computes the actual DAG, so use it instead of eyeballing:
130	  - **Broken edges** — `leandag build --json` reports `unknown_uses` (a `\uses{}` pointing at a label no declaration defines). Every one is a must-fix.
131	  - **Missing edges** — read each proof: if it relies on a lemma/definition that its `\uses{}` does not list, the edge is missing. These don't show up as "broken"; you find them by reading the math against the declared `\uses{}`. Missing edges make the loop dispatch provers out of order, so flag them as correctness findings.
132	  - **Isolated declarations** — `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated --type theorem`, and the `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint` counts in `leandag stats`) lists nodes with **no `\uses{}` out and nothing using them**. An isolated *blueprint* node is almost always a *symptom of a missing edge*, not dead weight — so for each one decide and record a disposition (see below). Isolated `lean_aux` nodes are uncovered Lean helpers, a separate "needs a blueprint entry" signal, not removal candidates.
133	- **Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)** — it is good practice for a chapter that relies on a Mathlib-provided result to state it as an explicit anchor block marked `\mathlibok` (with `\lean{}` naming the real Mathlib declaration), rather than a bare `\uses{}` to nothing. Treat such anchors as *valid and done* — never flag them as ∞ holes or `remove` candidates. But **audit each `\mathlibok` claim for faithfulness**, because a wrong one makes the loop skip a real gap: does Mathlib actually contain the named `\lean{}` declaration, and does the stated form match it? Verify against Lean (`archon-lean-lsp`) when in doubt. A `\mathlibok` on a statement Mathlib does not provide — or whose form is stronger/different than Mathlib's — is a hard fail (treat like a fabricated citation). Also: where a broken `\uses{}` points at what is really a Mathlib result, the right `wire-up` is "add a `\mathlibok` anchor for it," not "invent a project lemma."
134	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
135	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
136	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
137	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
138	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
139	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
140	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
141	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
142	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
143	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
144	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
145	
146	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
147	
148	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
149	
150	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
151	
152	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
153	
154	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
155	
156	## Always read everything
157	
158	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
159	
160	## Unstarted-phase proposals
161	
162	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
163	
164	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. For each phase row whose `Status` is not `complete` and whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
165	
166	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
167	
168	The proposal answers:
169	
170	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
171	
172	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
173	
174	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
175	
176	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
177	
178	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
179	
180	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
181	
182	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
183	
184	```
185	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
186	
187	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
188	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
189	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
190	
191	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
192	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
193	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
194	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
195	...
196	
197	**`\uses` skeleton**:
198	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
199	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
200	
201	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
202	
203	**References for writer**:
204	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
205	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
206	
207	**Subphase choices exposed**:
208	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
209	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
210	```
211	
212	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
213	
214	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
215	
216	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.

243	## What you do
244	
245	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
246	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
247	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
248	   - Read the entire chapter.
249	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
250	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
251	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
252	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
253	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
254	6. **Audit the dependency graph with `leandag`.** Run `leandag build --json` (broken/unknown `\uses{}`, `unmatched_lean`, isolated count) and `leandag show isolated` once for the whole blueprint. Then triage each isolated **blueprint** node into exactly one disposition and record it:
255	   - **wire-up** — it *should* connect to the graph but an edge is missing or mislabeled. Name the `\uses{}` that should be added (and on which side). This is the common case and a correctness finding — recommend a writer fix, do NOT recommend removal.
256	   - **remove** — it is genuinely orphaned scaffolding: nothing in the goal's proof closure needs it, it isn't the goal, and it isn't a `\mathlibok`/reference anchor. Only then is removal the right call. Name the chapter + label so the plan agent can authorize a writer to delete it.
257	   - **keep** — it is intentionally standalone (the goal theorem with nothing above it, a deliberately isolated `\mathlibok` reference, etc.). Say why and move on.
258	   You **flag and recommend**; you never edit. The plan agent turns a `wire-up`/`remove` disposition into a blueprint-writer directive.
259	7. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
260	8. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
261	9. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
262	
263	You may also use:
264	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
265	- **`leandag`** — your read-only window into the real dependency DAG. Run it freely: it never touches project source or the blueprint. (`leandag build` refreshes the derived `.leandag/` cache, which is regenerated deterministically from the current tree — that is a tool artifact, not a project edit, so it does not violate your read-only rule.)
266	  - `leandag build --json` — the structured build report: `unknown_uses` (broken `\uses{}`), `unmatched_lean` (`\lean{}` pointing nowhere), `conflicts`, and `summary.isolated`.
267	  - `leandag stats` — counts including `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint`.
268	  - `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated [--type theorem] [--chapter <c>]`) — the isolated declarations, which you triage per node (below).
269	  - `leandag show gaps` — blueprint nodes missing a `\lean{}`.
270	
271	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.

273	## Report format
274	
275	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
276	
277	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
278	
279	Concretely:
280	
281	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline`, `### Dependency & isolation findings` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all six are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
282	- `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals`: omit only when every phase in the strategy has adequate blueprint coverage. When present, this section is never empty — each proposal block is a concrete outline, not a one-line flag.
283	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
284	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.
285	- `## Per-chapter` blocks for clean chapters: when a chapter is `complete: true`, `correct: true`, and `notes` is empty, render it as a single compact line — `### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex — complete + correct, no notes.` — NOT a multi-line block with `notes: -` filler. Reserve full multi-line blocks for chapters that have actual findings.
286	- `## Severity summary`: when there are zero must-fix-this-iter and zero soon-severity items, render as `Severity summary: HARD GATE CLEARS — no findings.` and skip the per-tier breakdown.
287	
288	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.

390	
391	Apply these rules verbatim:
392	
393	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
394	  - The "Strategy-modifying findings" section is non-empty.
395	  - A route under "Multi-route coverage" is reported as MISSING.
396	  - **Any chapter has `complete: partial | false` OR `correct: partial | false`** — even if the strategy "does not require" that chapter this iter.
397	  - **Any chapter whose `\lean{...}` hint** is marked "Lean difficulty quality: poor" AND the named target is part of an active prover route in PROGRESS.md.
398	  - **Blueprint purity violations** — any chapter containing Lean syntax or project history must be cleaned before provers run.
399	  - **Broken `\uses{}` cross-references** that point at non-existent labels.
400	  - **Missing `\uses{}` edges** (a `wire-up` disposition) on a chapter feeding an active prover route — out-of-order dispatch risk.
401	  - **Unfaithful `\mathlibok` anchors** — a `\mathlibok` block whose `\lean{}` Mathlib declaration doesn't exist or whose form is stronger/different than Mathlib's. Like a fabricated citation, this hides a real gap from the loop; always must-fix.
402	  - **Citation-discipline findings on blocks feeding an active prover route**.
403	  - **Unstarted-phase proposals** — each proposed chapter lands here as `unstarted-phase proposal: <phase name> — dispatch blueprint-writer for <proposed chapter filename> or record deferral`. These are must-act because an unwritten blueprint is an unparallelisable, unpreviewable phase; writing it early is the cheapest action in the loop.
404	- **soon** — cross-cutting items that don't block any specific chapter's prover work yet:

9	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
10	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
11	
12	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
13	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
14	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
15	\[
16	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
17	  \qquad
18	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
19	\]
20	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
21	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
22	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
23	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
24	
25	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
26	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
27	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
28	% groups)
29	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
30	% SOURCE QUOTE:
31	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
32	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
33	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
34	% abelian groups defined by the formula
35	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
36	% \end{dfn}
37	%  [\dots]
38	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
39	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
40	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
41	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
42	% topologies  by
43	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
44	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
45	%  \end{dfn}"
46	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
47	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
48	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
49	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
50	homomorphisms.}
51	
52	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
53	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
54	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
55	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
56	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
57	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
58	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
59	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
60	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
61	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
62	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
63	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
64	structure. This is the content of
65	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
66	
67	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
68	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
69	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
70	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
71	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
72	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
73	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
74	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
75	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
76	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
77	\begin{enumerate}
78	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
79	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
80	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
81	    isomorphism classes;
82	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
83	    for \(\otimes\);
84	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
85	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
86	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
87	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
88	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
89	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
90	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
91	\end{enumerate}
92	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
93	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
94	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
95	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
96	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
97	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
98	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
99	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
100	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
101	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
102	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
103	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
104	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
105	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
106	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
107	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
108	supplied.
109	
110	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
111	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
112	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
113	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
114	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
115	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
116	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
117	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
118	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
119	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
120	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
121	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
122	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
123	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
124	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
125	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
126	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
127	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
128	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
129	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
130	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
131	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
132	avoids. The
133	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
134	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
135	The \emph{full} \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) coherent
136	monoidal category is therefore not built for the group law and is off the critical
137	path; what the group law does still need from this circle of ideas is the
138	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), realized as a
139	sheafification transport whose single open left-whisker obligation is closed by the
140	stalk--tensor commutation (d.2). The stalk apparatus (d.1/d.2) is accordingly
141	\emph{on} the critical path, while the coherent monoidal category itself is not.
142	
143	\section{Mathlib API survey}
144	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
145
